textPlain: One Apple platforms, you also want to use F_FULLFSYNC instead of fsync:https://www.sqlite.org/pragma.html#pragma_checkpoint_fullfsy...

One of the things I like about SQLite is how easy it is to understand its behavior if you read the docs. This is one of the potentially surprising defaults - but it seems reasonable for an embedded database, where a power loss is likely to cause the application to "forget" that something was committed at the same time that SQLite does.However, it depends on the application - hence the need for clear docs and an understandable model.

Does it matter? For all we know, it keeps the serializability. At this point (of computer hardware history), you would care more about serializability than making sure data written to disk after power loss, the latter would now depend on so many layers of drivers doing correct things (for things that is hard to test correctly).
I don't know why SQLite has this default, I suspect primarily performance, but I think even with modern SSD's there is a fsync frequency where it starts to hurt lifetime.
